annotations.label: Numerical stability

anchor.snippet: numerical stability is a generally desirable property of numerical algorithms

annotations.note: Mathlib does not develop a theory of numerical stability of algorithms.

annotations.label: Numerically stable algorithm

anchor.snippet: Calculations that can be proven not to magnify approximation errors are called numerically stable

annotations.note: No definition of numerical stability of an algorithm exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Forward error

anchor.snippet: The forward error of the algorithm is the difference between the result and the "true" solution

annotations.note: Mathlib has no notion of forward error for numerical algorithms.

annotations.label: Backward error

anchor.snippet: The backward error is the smallest

annotations.note: Mathlib has no notion of backward error for numerical algorithms.

annotations.label: Forward-backward error bound via condition number

anchor.snippet: the forward error is at most as big in magnitude as the condition number multiplied by the magnitude of the backward error

annotations.note: Mathlib lacks the condition-number and error notions required to state this inequality.

annotations.label: Backward stable

anchor.snippet: The algorithm is said to be backward stable if the backward error is small for all inputs

annotations.note: Backward stability of algorithms is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Mixed stability

anchor.snippet: a more general concept, called mixed stability

annotations.note: Mixed stability of numerical algorithms is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Backward stable implies stable

anchor.snippet: Hence, a backward stable algorithm is always stable

annotations.note: This implication cannot be stated since neither notion is in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Forward stable

anchor.snippet: An algorithm is forward stable if its forward error divided by the condition number of the problem is small

annotations.note: Forward stability is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: A-stability

anchor.snippet: various concepts of numerical stability exist, for instance A-stability

annotations.note: Mathlib has no numerical ODE solver theory, hence no A-stability.

annotations.label: Stability for linear evolutionary PDE algorithms

anchor.snippet: An algorithm for solving a linear evolutionary partial differential equation is stable if the total variation of the numerical solution at a fixed time remains bounded as the step size goes to zero

annotations.note: Mathlib has no numerical PDE framework in which to state this stability notion.

annotations.label: Lax equivalence theorem

anchor.snippet: The Lax equivalence theorem states that an algorithm converges if it is consistent and stable

annotations.note: The Lax equivalence theorem from numerical analysis is not in Mathlib (the `LaxMilgram` file is unrelated).

annotations.label: Babylonian method vs Method X for sqrt(2)
annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize the Babylonian iteration or compare it to other floating-point schemes for √2.
